essential_question: How do you put one exact number on how far apart two rays have turned from the same starting point?
objective: Name an angle by its vertex and sides (∠ABC, ∠B, ∠1), and use a protractor to measure and draw angles to the nearest whole degree.

content.prompt: Hold up two fingers like an open pair of scissors, both starting from the same base — that base is a vertex. Without a ruler, how could you put one exact number on how far open they are? And what would you actually be measuring — the fingers' length, or something else?

content.heading: Two rays, one shared endpoint, one number for the turn
bullets: An angle is formed by two rays that share a common endpoint — the vertex.
Name it three ways: ∠ABC with the vertex letter in the middle, ∠B alone (only if B is the vertex of just one angle in the figure), or ∠1 as a numbered label.
m∠ABC is read 'the measure of angle ABC' — the number of degrees in the angle's opening, from 0° to 360°.
The two rays are the angle's sides; the point where they meet is its vertex.
Two opposite rays (forming one straight line) make a straight angle, measuring exactly 180° — and a quarter of that turn, a right angle, measures exactly 90°.

agenda.title: Explore — Drag the Ray, Read the Degree

content.heading: Turn the ray; watch the degree change
content.note: On the sim, drag the gold handle around vertex V. Watch m∠AVB update live as you turn ray VB. Stop exactly at 90° — what do you notice? Then try 180°. Switch to 'Draw to Target' mode and try to land within a couple degrees of a random target.

content.preview: Build session: hunt real-world angles and draw a set of exact target angles with a real protractor.

success_criteria.text: I can name an angle by its vertex and sides, using ∠ABC, ∠B, or ∠1 correctly
I can measure an angle with a protractor to the nearest whole degree
I can draw an angle of a given measure using a protractor

questions.prompt: The shared endpoint of the two rays that form an angle is called the…
questions.explain: The vertex is the single point where both rays of an angle begin.
'm∠' always means 'the measure of' the angle that follows — a number of degrees, not the angle figure itself.

questions.prompt: The figure shows ∠AVB. Use a real protractor on this printed page to measure it to the nearest whole degree. Write m∠AVB.
questions.explain: Rubric: center the protractor on V, align 0° with ray VB (or VA), and read where the other ray crosses the scale — accept 23°-27° for print/measurement tolerance.

questions.prompt: On the grid, use a protractor to draw ∠XYZ with m∠XYZ = 50°. Label the vertex Y and mark both rays X and Z.
questions.answer: Two rays from a labeled vertex Y opening to 50°, with points X and Z marked on the rays.
questions.explain: Rubric: a vertex Y with two rays drawn from it (1 pt) + the opening between the rays measuring 50° when checked with a protractor (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Write two correct names for an angle whose vertex is point B, with sides passing through A and C.
questions.answer: ∠ABC or ∠CBA (the vertex letter always goes in the middle); ∠B is also correct if B is the vertex of only one angle in the figure.
questions.explain: Any three-letter name works as long as the vertex sits in the middle; the single-letter form only works when there's no ambiguity about which angle at B is meant.

questions.prompt: Two opposite rays sharing an endpoint form a ___ angle, measuring exactly 180°.
questions.explain: Opposite rays rebuild one unbroken straight line, so the angle between them is a straight angle — 180°.

questions.prompt: Exit: find one angle around you right now (a laptop screen, a door, a corner) and estimate its measure in degrees. Is it closer to a small slice of a circle or a big one?
